narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the resilience it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes a solid LSAT score of 36, which is right around the typical score for students in our dataset, an undergraduate GPA of 2.4 that's below the average of 3.21, early-year school grades placing you in the second decile out of ten, and later-year grades also in the second decile compared to the usual fifth or sixth, along with a middle-class family income and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your risk of failing at 46.3%—a notably high probability that tipped the scales toward rejection to protect the school's bar passage rates. The key reason for this decision was your position in the lower deciles for school grades, especially in your third year, which had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure by signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong academic performance. Your early-year grades also nudged the prediction in that direction, though with less force. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out as the biggest factor pushing toward success, effectively countering some of those concerns and showing your aptitude for the rigors ahead. Your middle-class background and full-time enrollment plans provided smaller but positive influences, aligning with traits of students who typically pass, while your undergraduate GPA offered a modest boost too. Compared to typical students who get approved, your school deciles are lower than the middle-of-the-pack averages, which often correlate with smoother bar outcomes, even though your LSAT holds up well. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman trailblazer—it's just one model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA-enhancing prep if possible, and reapplying with strengthened academics; many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact areas. We're rooting for you—law needs your perspective.
